title: Family History Interview Preparation: The Foxfire Approach
description: Learn how to prepare for and conduct a family history interview using the Foxfire methodology. Understand how to identify the right person to interview, prepare meaningful questions, listen carefully, and create a record that preserves the family history for future generations.

steps.instruction: What is a family history interview? A family history interview is a structured conversation with a family member or community elder to capture their knowledge of family history, community events, and lived experience. The Foxfire approach treats the interviewer as a student and the elder as the teacher — the goal is to record what the elder knows, not to confirm what the interviewer already thinks.
steps.culturalContext: The Foxfire project, begun in 1966 by Eliot Wigginton and students at Rabun Gap-Nacoochee School in Georgia, demonstrated that the most valuable historical sources are often sitting in the same room as the researcher. Appalachian elders interviewed in the 1960s–1970s described building techniques, food preservation methods, and community events that had no written record anywhere else. Their testimony became primary source material for scholars and practitioners alike. The same principle applies to family history: the stories that seem ordinary to the teller may be extraordinary to future generations.

steps.instruction: Identifying who to interview: start with the oldest family members who are still able to participate. In Appalachian oral tradition, family knowledge often runs through grandmothers and great-aunts — women who maintained the genealogy and kept the family Bible. Ask: who knows the most about where our family came from? Who tells the family stories? Who remembers the most names and dates?
steps.culturalContext: The family Bible is an underutilized genealogical resource in Appalachian research. Many Appalachian families recorded births, deaths, and marriages in the front pages of the family Bible across multiple generations. If your family has an old Bible, photograph all the handwritten pages before anything else — these records are irreplaceable. Church records (baptism and burial registers), courthouse records (deed books, will books, marriage registers), and census records (publicly available for 1790–1940) are the documentary context that can corroborate and extend oral accounts.

steps.instruction: Preparing questions: organize your questions in layers. Start with simple facts (names, dates, places) to establish trust and a basic framework. Then move to stories (what happened when, what did it feel like, who was there). Finally ask for reflections (what was most important, what do you want remembered). Do not lead the answers — ask open questions and listen to where the story goes.

The interview environment: choose a quiet, comfortable setting. Bring a recording device (with explicit permission) and a notebook. Do not let the recording device substitute for your attention — look at the person, respond to their pauses and emphases, ask follow-up questions when something important comes up. Review your recording promptly after the interview while your memory is fresh.
steps.culturalContext: The quality of an oral history interview depends heavily on the interviewer's presence and attention. Elders who feel genuinely heard often share more than they planned to. Elders who feel they are performing for a recording device often retreat to polished, well-rehearsed summaries. Your presence is the most important recording instrument in the room.

steps.instruction: Handling family memory gaps: what do you do when the elder says 'I don't know' or 'I can't remember'? These are valuable data points, not failures. Note them. Also note when memories are uncertain ('I think it was about 1940') versus confident ('It was definitely before the flood of 1937'). Uncertainty is not a defect in oral history — it is information about the reliability of the record. An elder who says 'I'm not sure, but I believe it was my grandmother who first came over the mountain' is giving you a direction for further research.

Preserving and sharing: transcribe the recording (or detailed notes) promptly. Create a written record with: date of interview, name of person interviewed, context (why this interview, what you knew beforehand), summary, and verbatim quotes for the most important material. Share the record back with the person you interviewed — let them correct errors and approve what you have recorded. Make copies for family members.
steps.culturalContext: The OCAP principle — that community members own their own stories — applies to family history just as to Indigenous oral history. The person you interviewed should have a copy of what you recorded. If they want to change something, honor that. The oral history belongs to the teller, not to the researcher. A family timeline that lives only on one person's computer is more vulnerable than one shared with the community it represents.
